schools.summary: At Iconium the apostles entered the synagogue together and so spake that a great multitude of both Jews and Greeks believed — following their regular method (Henry), the effective preaching winning a wide harvest beyond Israel; success and opposition go together (cf. v.2).

themes: went into the synagogue of the Jews
a great multitude of Jews and Greeks believed

schools.summary: The unbelieving Jews stirred up and embittered the Gentiles against the brethren — the Jewish origin of nearly all the persecutions in Acts (Ellicott); poisoning potential hearers into enemies (Robertson).
Calvin: the Jews like firebrands inflaming the Gentiles; the irony that the gospel of peace becomes, through men's perversity, an occasion of tumults.

themes: the unbelieving Jews stirred up the Gentiles

schools.summary: The apostles abode long, undaunted by persecution because of God's success (Barnes); the miracles came as the confirmation of faith, not its foundation (Ellicott); the Lord bore witness to his own word of grace (Robertson).
Calvin: the true use of miracles — never separated from the word, tending only to establish the gospel's authority, against the Papists who lead men astray by bare miracles.

debates.question: What is the purpose of miracles?

sides.position: To confirm the word/faith, never as its foundation, never separated from the gospel

themes: gave testimony unto the word of his grace
signs and wonders confirming the word
miracles never separated from the word

schools.summary: The city divided — a schism within the Gentile populace (Robertson); 'the apostles,' first used here of Paul and Barnabas, not in the technical sense of the Twelve but the broader sense of those sent (Robertson).

Calvin: the division flowed from the gospel yet was not its fault; the Spirit pronounces it to the apostles' praise — better to divide from the world and come to God than to have peace with the world at God's cost.

themes: the multitude of the city was divided
part with the Jews, part with the apostles

schools.summary: An assault planned — Gentiles and Jews with their (synagogue) rulers (Robertson) — to insult and stone the apostles; the intended stoning showing the Jews in the lead; Paul, once a persecutor (1 Timothy 1:13), now received what he dealt (Ellicott).
Calvin: the apostles' courage and prudence — not fleeing at mere opposition, but avoiding the fury when in danger of stoning, fleeing not from fear but in wisdom.

themes: an assault to use them despitefully and stone them
Jews and Gentiles with their rulers
the threat that drove them to flee

schools.summary: Aware of the plot, the apostles fled to Lystra and Derbe in Lycaonia — a literal obedience to Christ's command (Matthew 10:23, Ellicott), the wise preacher knowing when to leave (Robertson); the move into the wild 'Wolf-land' of Lycaonia, where Paul first preached to pure heathen with no synagogue (Ellicott).

At Lystra and Derbe the apostles preached the gospel — not resting or hiding but continuing their work (Gill), with extensive evangelism perhaps aided by disciples from the earlier cities (Robertson); the constancy of the faithful evangelist, persecution moving them to a new field.
A man lame from birth, who had never walked, sat at Lystra where Paul preached — a lifelong, hopeless affliction beyond natural cure, the well-known condition magnifying the miracle to come.
Henry: the parallel with Peter's miracle (Acts 3) — as that introduced the gospel among the Jews, so Paul's healing here introduces it among the Gentiles.

themes: a certain man at Lystra, impotent in his feet
a cripple from his mother's womb

schools.summary: The lame man heard Paul (faith coming by hearing, Clarke), having been among his hearers for days (Ellicott); Paul, fixing his gaze (as in 13:9, Robertson), perceived the faith God had wrought in the man's earnest upward look.
Calvin: a special gift-faith for the miracle, not the ordinary saving faith — a peculiar preparation revealed to Paul by the Spirit, not a common rule.

themes: faith the condition of the miracle

schools.summary: Paul, with a loud voice (that all might hear, Calvin), commanded the cripple to stand, and he leaped up and walked — the immediate, complete cure parallel to Peter's miracle (Acts 3:8, Robertson); the two tenses marking a single bound then continued walking (Ellicott).
Calvin: many MSS add 'in the name of the Lord Jesus' — the apostles careful to magnify the name of Christ in all miracles; the loud voice that the sudden change might move the multitude.

themes: the cure in the name of Christ
the power of God among the heathen

schools.summary: The people cried in Lycaonian that 'the gods are come down in the likeness of men' — shaped by the local legend of Zeus and Hermes (Ellicott, from Ovid); Ellicott's crucial inference: the apostles did not understand the speech, proving the gift of tongues was not a supernatural knowledge of every dialect.
Calvin: man's bent to superstition — the people ascribed the miracle to their idols, catching at the wonder while neglecting the doctrine, the root of all superstition.

debates.question: Did the gift of tongues include supernatural knowledge of every local dialect?

sides.position: No — the Lystra incident is a crucial instance showing the apostles did not understand Lycaonian

themes: saying in the speech of Lycaonia
the gods are come down in the likeness of men
the pagan response to the miracle

schools.summary: The Lystrans called Barnabas Jupiter (Zeus, the more imposing, Chrysostom's conjecture, Barnes, Ellicott) and Paul Mercury (Hermes), the god of eloquence and messenger, 'because he was the chief speaker' (Robertson); the identification from the local mythology and the apostles' appearance and role.

themes: because he was the chief speaker

schools.summary: The priest of Jupiter (the guardian deity whose temple stood before the city — Luke's precise phrase matching inscriptions, Clarke, Ellicott) brought garlanded oxen to sacrifice to the apostles; the sacrificial customs noted from classical parallels (Barnes, Clarke).
Calvin: the priest's covetousness — scenting profit if it were noised that Jupiter had appeared, he led the people to sacrifice; superstition fed by self-interest.

themes: the priest of Jupiter before the city

schools.summary: The apostles, hearing of the intended sacrifice (not grasped at first, the cry being in Lycaonian), rent their clothes (the Jewish expression of horror at blasphemy) and rushed in to stop the idolatry — a standing rebuke to all who accept divine honours.
Chrysostom: the vehemence — a grief inconsolable that they should be thought gods and introduce idolatry, the very thing they came to destroy.

themes: they rent their clothes, and ran in

schools.summary: The apostles refused worship ('men of like passions') and proclaimed the living God, Maker of all; Barnes draws the great contrast — the apostles abhorred the worship Christ accepted (John 20:28), a proof of Christ's deity; the idols called 'vanities' (the Jewish term, Ellicott).
Calvin: the method of preaching to pagans — beginning with the one Creator (near to common sense) before Christ; the principle that all religion departing from God's word is vain.

themes: we also are men of like passions with you
turn from these vanities unto the living God
the contrast with Christ accepting worship

schools.summary: God 'suffered all nations [the Gentiles] to walk in their own ways' — Paul's philosophy of history, the times of ignorance permitted by God (Ellicott, cf. Acts 17:30; Romans 1); God withdrawing the restraints of grace (Robertson), the experiment of reason proving man's depravity and the need of revelation (Barnes).

Calvin: the apostles take away the cloak of long antiquity and universal consent — the nations' long blindness no argument against the now-revealed truth, but a proof of reason's impotence without God's word.

themes: suffered all nations to walk in their own ways

schools.summary: God 'left not himself without witness' — the witness of nature, his goodness in rain, harvest, food, and gladness (the germ of Romans 1, Ellicott); the rhythmic close suggesting a quoted hymn (Ellicott); Paul seeing the living God behind the physical world, not self-governing laws (Robertson).
Calvin: natural revelation leaves men without excuse yet does not suffice for salvation, which comes by the word — faith conceived not by beholding heaven and earth but by hearing the word.

debates.question: What is the scope of God's witness in nature to the heathen?

sides.position: Sufficient to leave men without excuse (Romans 1), but not sufficient for salvation, which comes by the word

themes: he left not himself without witness
rain from heaven and fruitful seasons

schools.summary: The apostles scarce restrained the people from sacrificing — a harrowing ordeal, the people so convinced the gods had appeared (Barnes, Robertson); yet some may have been truly converted, including Timothy and his family (2 Timothy 1:5, Ellicott).
Calvin: the madness of idolatry — the people would not believe the apostles' word though they believed them gods, the obstinacy of superstition.

themes: that they had not done sacrifice

schools.summary: Jews from Antioch and Iconium persuaded the fickle crowd, who stoned Paul and dragged him out for dead — a sudden reversal from worship to stoning (Chrysostom), in the very city where he had nearly been worshipped; the Jewish malice pursuing the apostles beyond their own cities; Paul receiving what he dealt Stephen (7:58).

themes: having stoned Paul, drew him out of the city

schools.summary: Paul rose and entered the city, then went to Derbe; the witnesses marvel at his courage in returning to the city where he was stoned; Timothy, perhaps present, would never forget it (Robertson, 2 Timothy 3:11).
Clarke, Wesley (with Calvin): a miraculous recovery, near a resurrection — that he journeyed fresh and sound the next day proving the miracle.

debates.question: Was Paul's recovery from the stoning miraculous or natural?

sides.position: Miraculous, near a resurrection — he journeyed fresh and sound the next day
A natural recovery from a stunning blow — the text does not assert a miracle

themes: he rose up, and came into the city

schools.summary: The apostles preached at Derbe and made many disciples (obeying Christ's command to disciple the nations, Robertson), then courageously returned through Lystra, Iconium, and Antioch — the very cities of persecution, deeming the welfare of the infant churches above their own safety (Barnes, Clarke).

themes: preached the gospel to Derbe, taught many
returned again to Lystra, Iconium, Antioch
the pastoral heart of the mission

schools.summary: The apostles strengthened the disciples, teaching 'we must through much tribulation enter the kingdom'; 'confirming' not the ecclesiastical rite but strengthening by the truth (Barnes, Ellicott); the 'we' perhaps reflecting Luke's hearing or a general truth (2 Timothy 3:12, Robertson).

Calvin: the Christian life is warfare, requiring preparation for tribulation; yet tribulation is not meritorious but the way God handles his own — against the Papist reading of patience as deserving salvation.

themes: confirming the souls of the disciples
through much tribulation enter the kingdom
tribulation not meritorious but the appointed path

schools.summary: The apostles appointed elders (= bishops, on the synagogue model, Robertson) with prayer and fasting; 'ordained' (cheirotoneo) properly means election by show of hands (Barnes, Clarke distinguishing it from ordination by imposition of hands); Ellicott holds the apostles simply appointed.
Calvin (with Robertson): the people elected by suffrage while the apostles presided as moderators; the prayer and fasting for God's direction in choosing and equipping the pastors.

debates.question: Did 'ordained' (cheirotoneo) mean the people elected the elders or the apostles appointed them?

sides.position: The people elected by show of hands, the apostles presiding
The apostles appointed them (the word used of the apostles' act)

themes: ordained them elders in every church

schools.summary: The apostles passed through Pisidia to Pamphylia (Robertson), continuing the homeward journey and preaching where opportunity offered (Calvin); a stage in the return, retracing the outward route toward the coast and home.

The apostles preached at Perga (doing on the return what they had left undone outward-bound, where John Mark departed, Ellicott) and went down to Attalia, the port of Pamphylia, to sail home; the apostles neglecting no opportunity to preach.

themes: the port for the voyage home

schools.summary: The apostles sailed to Antioch, whence they had been commended to grace for the work now fulfilled (referring to the sending, 13:1-3, Barnes); the 'work fulfilled' the missionary tour, not an ordination to the ministry or apostleship but a temporary commission completed (Barnes, Wesley).
Calvin: the whole journey and success ascribed to God's grace — their preaching no man's work but a work of the grace of God.

themes: recommended to the grace of God

schools.summary: The apostles gathered the church and reported 'all that God had done with them' — the first missionary meeting in history (Robertson), ascribing all to God (Calvin); 'the door of faith' Paul's characteristic metaphor (Ellicott, 1 Corinthians 16:9), the Gentile door now opened.
Calvin: the door opened not only by external preaching but by the effectual inward call — no man enters save he whom God draws by the Spirit.

themes: rehearsed all that God had done with them
opened the door of faith unto the Gentiles

schools.summary: The apostles abode a long time with the disciples at Antioch — a season of rest, fellowship, and ministry after the labours and dangers of the journey (the great city needing teachers, Chrysostom); the close of the first missionary journey, before the Jerusalem council (ch. 15).

themes: abode long time with the disciples
the close of the first journey
